title: Running pigs in woodland
subtitle: How pigs interact with woodland, what they can be fed there legally, and what the ground tells you after they leave.
excerpt: Woodland grazing for pigs is productive and welfare-positive but it is not set-and-forget. Pigs root hard and will clear ground cover within weeks if stocked too heavily or left too long. Rotation through woodland plots, combined with understanding of what pigs can and cannot eat there legally, turns a woodland block into a useful part of the smallholding rotation.
sourceNotes: DEFRA: guidance on feeding pigs, gov.uk. Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA): swill feeding regulations. Woodland Trust: pigs in woodland conservation management. Rare Breeds Survival Trust: traditional pig keeping in woodland.

glossaryTerms.definition: The historical practice of releasing pigs into woodland in autumn to feed on fallen mast (acorns, beech mast, and other tree fruits). Still practised in the New Forest and on traditional smallholdings. Pigs in pannage condition rapidly on acorns and convert the fat they lay down from the high tannin mast into a distinctive, firm back fat prized in traditional pork and charcuterie.

The legal prohibition in England, Scotland, and Wales on feeding catering waste, kitchen scraps, or any food that has been in contact with meat to pigs. Introduced after the 2001 foot-and-mouth disease outbreak. Feeding swill to pigs is a criminal offence. Pigs may be fed garden fruit and vegetables grown on the holding, provided they have not been in contact with meat or come from a catering kitchen.

Overconsumption of green or unripe acorns can cause kidney damage and digestive upset in pigs. Fully ripe, fallen acorns in moderate quantities are safe and nutritious. Management in pannage involves ensuring pigs have access to other feed sources alongside mast, rather than relying solely on acorns during a heavy mast year.

content.text: Woodland in autumn is a highly productive environment for pigs. The mast drop from oak, beech, and sweet chestnut delivers energy-dense feed at no cost, and pigs benefit from the rooting and exploratory behaviour the habitat encourages. Woodland pig keeping has three constraints: what you can legally feed them, rotation to avoid permanent ground damage, and breed selection.

runs pigs in woodland through September and October as the acorn and beech mast drops. Traditional breeds condition rapidly on mast; the fat they lay down has a different flavour and texture than pigs fed only on cereals. For smallholders selling pork direct, pannage-finished pigs are a clear quality differentiator.

in years of heavy acorn drop when pigs eat little else. Green or unripe acorns carry higher tannin levels than fully ripe fallen mast. Supplement with a daily ration of compound feed or grain to ensure pigs are not relying exclusively on acorns during the transition into the woodland plot.

applies regardless of where pigs are kept. You may feed pigs fruit and vegetables grown on the holding, but not kitchen scraps, catering waste, or any material that has been in contact with meat. In a woodland context, this means you can bring garden windfalls and root crops to the woodland pen, but not the contents of a food waste bin. The mast itself, fallen on the ground, is unrestricted. Water must always be available; woodland acreage does not substitute for a permanent drinker.

Pigs ruin a woodland plot quickly if stocked at the wrong density or left for too long in one area. Ground flora, root systems of young trees, and the soil structure of a mature woodland floor are damaged permanently by repeated, heavy pig pressure. A useful guide is to move pigs when ground cover is around 50% depleted rather than waiting until the area is bare mud. Plots that are rested for at least two growing seasons recover well; rested for one season they partially recover. A rotation of three or four plots allows sustainable year-on-year use of the same woodland area.

Fence woodland plots with an electric fence rather than permanent pig wire: it lets you move boundaries easily, is cheaper to establish, and the wire can be retrieved when the plot rests. Two strands, at 20 cm and 40 cm height, are standard for outdoor pig electric fencing.
